context.description: The page reflects on self-observation during speech, shame as a reaction to inner contradiction, and the deliberate narrowing of awareness to stay functional. It uses Ouspensky as an example and compares expanding awareness to weight training and a rubber band that snaps back to comfort.
normalization_notes: Mapped raw publication/book to philosophy-focused book page.
No visible chapter heading or page title was available.

rows.paragraph_text: Ouspensky is cited as an example of remembering a moment when one hears oneself talking and becomes a detached observer of one’s own speech and the reactions of others.
rows.text_content: Ouspensky is cited as an example of remembering a moment when one hears oneself talking and becomes a detached observer of one’s own speech and the reactions of others.

rows.paragraph_text: Shame arises because inner contradiction becomes consciously visible, so awareness is usually kept at a comfortable level to avoid pain and loss of function.
rows.text_content: Shame arises because inner contradiction becomes consciously visible, so awareness is usually kept at a comfortable level to avoid pain and loss of function.

rows.paragraph_text: Expanding awareness is compared to weight training and a rubber band that snaps back to its starting position unless it is deliberately stretched further.
rows.text_content: Expanding awareness is compared to weight training and a rubber band that snaps back to its starting position unless it is deliberately stretched further.

content.free_text: book_page nonfiction_philosophy consciousness_essay Book page on consciousness and self-observation. Ouspensky example, shame from inner contradiction, limited awareness, rubber band and weight training metaphor.
